18.text: JAX: Google's functional autodiff framework. A completely different philosophy from PyTorch — no classes, no hidden state, pure functions everywhere.

19.text: jnp is JAX's NumPy replacement — same API, but arrays live on GPU/TPU and support automatic differentiation.

20.text: grad transforms a function into its gradient function. jit compiles Python into optimized machine code. These are JAX's core transformations.

52.text: JAX's PRNG is explicit — you create a key and split it for each random operation. No hidden global state, unlike NumPy/PyTorch.

55.text: Parameter initializer — same N(0, 0.08) distribution, but using JAX's RNG.

56.text: JAX random requires an explicit key for every call. This makes randomness reproducible and parallelizable.

59.text: Pre-split 20 random keys for parameter initialization. JAX forces you to be explicit about where randomness enters the computation.

60.text: Iterator over keys — each parameter gets its own unique key.

62.text: Parameters are a plain Python dict — no nn.Module, no parameter registration. In JAX, parameters are just data you pass to functions.

67.text: Per-layer weights — same structure as previous labs, just stored in a dict.

75.text: jax.tree.leaves extracts all arrays from the nested dict. JAX treats dicts as "pytrees" — a core abstraction for working with nested parameter structures.

82.text: RMSNorm as a bare function — no class, no state. JAX style: functions in, functions out.

83.text: Mean of squares, same math as every other lab.

87.text: The forward pass is a PURE FUNCTION: same inputs always produce the same outputs, no side effects. This is what makes jax.grad and jax.jit work — they need mathematical functions, not stateful objects.

93.text: Sequence length from the input shape.

94.text: Token embedding lookup via array indexing — same operation as PyTorch's nn.Embedding.

102.text: Matrix multiply for Q projection. Explicit @ with the parameter array — no hidden weight storage.

107.text: Multi-head reshape — same transpose pattern as PyTorch.

111.text: Scaled dot-product attention. Same math, different framework.

112.text: Causal mask using JAX's array ops.

113.text: Apply mask with jnp.where — JAX's equivalent of PyTorch's masked_fill.

117.text: Transpose and reshape heads back to (n, D).

118.text: Output projection + residual in one line.

131.text: Loss function — also a pure function. Takes params, inputs, and targets; returns a scalar loss. This is exactly what jax.grad expects.

133.text: Run the forward pass inside the loss function. grad will differentiate through this entire computation.

134.text: log_softmax is numerically more stable than separate softmax + log.

136.text: Gather target log-probs and negate their mean. Cross-entropy in one expression.

141.text: The JAX magic: grad(loss_fn) returns a NEW FUNCTION that computes gradients. jit compiles it. No backward pass code, no gradient tape — just function transformation.

142.text: JIT-compile the loss function too for fast evaluation.

147.text: Same hyperparameters as all previous labs.

148.text: jax.tree.map applies zeros_like to every array in the params dict. Functional alternative to manually creating matching zero buffers.

149.text: Second moment state — same pytree structure.

152.text: Adam update as a pure function — takes old state, returns new state. No mutation. This is the functional paradigm: transform data, don't change it.

153.text: Build new dicts rather than modifying in place.

157.text: First moment update — same formula as all previous labs.

161.text: Parameter update. Creates a NEW array; the old one is untouched.

162.text: Return the complete new state — params, first moments, second moments.

178.text: Compute gradients using the JIT-compiled gradient function. One call, and JAX's autodiff handles everything — same as PyTorch's loss.backward() but functional.

181.text: Functional Adam update. Returns entirely new params, new moments — nothing is mutated. The old state could be garbage collected.

191.text: JAX's PRNG needs explicit keys for every random operation during inference too.

195.text: Convert growing token list to JAX array.

196.text: Forward pass — the same pure function, just called without gradient tracking.

198.text: jax.random.split produces two new keys from one — JAX's way of consuming randomness without hidden state. Every random call needs a unique key.

199.text: categorical samples from log-probabilities (unnormalized logits). JAX's equivalent of PyTorch's multinomial.
